title: Build a Stack
prompt: A stack is last-in, first-out — like a stack of plates. Implement a Stack class with push(x), pop() (removes and returns the top), peek() (returns the top without removing), is_empty(), and support for len() (define __len__). The next few challenges build on this one.

details.restate: Build a Stack class — last in, first out. It needs: push(x) to put an item on top, pop() to remove AND give back the top item, peek() to look at the top without removing it, is_empty(), and len() support.

io.because: 3 went on last, so it comes off first
peek looks at the new top but leaves it in place

terms.meaning: last in, first out — the newest item is the first one removed

help.plain: A stack only ever adds to and removes from the SAME end — the top. The last thing you push is the first thing you pop. Store the items in a list and always work its end.
help.analogy: Like a stack of plates: you add to the top and take from the top. You never pull the bottom plate out.
help.mnemonic: LIFO — Last In, First Out. The most recently pushed item is the next one to leave.
help.nudge: A Python list already adds and removes at its end with `.append()` and `.pop()`. What should `peek()` return without removing anything?

example.problem: A tiny Counter class that goes up and down but never below zero (a different task — but the same 'class holds state, methods change it' shape).
example.note: Notice the pattern: __init__ sets up the internal data (here a number, for you a list), and each method reads or changes it. Your Stack keeps a list and its methods push/pop/peek its end.
